fact: Grain ships from Egypt carried the plague (and infected rats/fleas) to Constantinople
source: Procopius, History of the Wars, Book II, Chapters 22–23; also discussed in Plague and the End of Antiquity, Lester K. Little, ed. (Cambridge University Press, 2007)

fact: Constantinople imported its grain supply by sea, primarily from Egypt
source: The Cambridge Ancient History, Vol. XIV; also Procopius, The Secret History. Egypt (especially Alexandria) was the empire's breadbasket.

fact: Symptoms included swellings (buboes) in the groin and armpits
source: Procopius, History of the Wars, Book II, Chapter 22. Procopius describes bubonic swellings as a primary symptom.

fact: Five thousand people died every day at the plague's peak in Constantinople
source: Procopius, History of the Wars, II.23, reports deaths reaching 5,000 and then 10,000 per day. Modern historians debate exact figures but cite Procopius as the primary source.

fact: Justinian kept the harbor and grain shipments open despite the plague
source: This is a reasonable inference from the historical record — Constantinople never closed its harbors during the plague, and Justinian maintained the grain supply (the annona). However, no primary source records a specific decision by Justinian to refuse closing the harbor. Procopius and John of Ephesus document the ongoing crisis but not this exact refusal. The claim is a dramatic simplification.
